summary: The author combined distillation and inference techniques from previous competition winners, using Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct as a student model distilled from Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct teachers. Training leveraged a carefully curated mix of human preference datasets, external battle logs, and complexity-filtered synthetic data, with soft labels manually corrected for high-disagreement samples. The final submission was a linear merge of two student models, followed by a two-pass inference strategy that applied response-order swapping only to the most uncertain predictions. Key drivers of success included targeted test-time augmentation, strict preservation of language/domain distributions in synthetic data, and strategic dataset curation over brute-force scaling.
extensive_summary:
## Setup & Architecture
The solution frames the task as binary classification using a default `ForSequenceClassification` head with two output classes. Inputs are constructed by concatenating the prompt and both model responses, with proportional middle truncation applied when the formatted sample exceeds the maximum context length. During training, both response orders are included in a single gradient step to mitigate positional bias.

## Pretraining & Teacher Training
The pipeline begins with full-parameter fine-tuning of both `Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct` (student) and `Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct` (teacher) on external preference datasets (`mlabonne/orpo-dpo-mix-40k` and `opencsg/UltraFeedback-chinese`) using `DeepSpeed ZeRO3`. Training employs a linear decay learning rate schedule (50 warm-up steps, peak 4e-6) and a gradient clipping threshold of 64.0. Five teacher models are subsequently trained on 5 folds of the competition dataset and `lmarena-ai/arena-human-preference-55k` (80k samples after filtering ties, multi-turn responses, and deduplication), with model names prepended to each response.

## Distillation & Data Curation
Distillation leverages the five teachers to generate soft labels, with manual review and correction of samples exhibiting high disagreement with human annotations. The training mix expands to include ties, multi-turn first turns, `lmsys/chatbot_arena_conversations`, `lmarena-ai/PPE-Human-Preference-V1`, `lmarena-ai/Llama-3-70b-battles`, `lmarena-ai/gpt-4o-mini_battles`, external Kaggle datasets, and 50k synthetic samples. Synthetic data was generated by filtering prompts with complexity scores ≥3 (mirroring `Arena-hard` methodology) while preserving language and domain distributions, then uniformly sampling completions from several large models via `vLLM`. Losses combine cross-entropy (for original/hard labels), KL divergence, and Cosine loss on soft labels. The final model is a linear merge of a model trained on the full dataset and one excluding the last two datasets.

## Inference & Post-processing
Inference uses sequence unpadding adapted from a previous solution. A two-pass strategy is employed: the merged model processes all samples first, followed by a second pass with response order swapping using the original model on the 33% most uncertain predictions. This targeted approach balances computational cost with performance gains, avoiding the degradation seen in full-sample TTA.

## Key Takeaways
- Manual correction of high-disagreement soft labels significantly improves distillation quality.
- Including evaluation datasets in training can be beneficial if leakage or distribution correlation is suspected.
- Targeted TTA on uncertain samples outperforms full-sample augmentation when compute is constrained.
- Preserving language and domain distributions during synthetic data generation is critical for generalization.
competition_flow: Full-parameter fine-tuning of Qwen2.5-14B/72B on external preference data, distilling soft labels from five teacher models trained on curated human preference and synthetic datasets, merging two student models, and applying a two-pass inference strategy with targeted response-order swapping on uncertain samples.
data_processing: Filtering out ties and multi-turn responses
Concatenating prompt and responses with proportional middle truncation
Filtering synthetic prompts by complexity score (≥3) while preserving language/domain distributions
Uniformly sampling completions from specified models via vLLM
ensembling: The final model is a linear merge of two student models (one trained on the full dataset, one excluding the last two datasets), combined with a two-pass inference strategy that applies response-order swapping via the original model on the 33% most uncertain predictions.
insights: Manually reviewing and correcting high-disagreement soft-labeled samples significantly improves distillation quality.
Including evaluation datasets in the training mix can be beneficial if there's a risk of data leakage or if they correlate with the test distribution.
Targeted test-time augmentation on only the most uncertain samples balances computational cost with performance gains better than applying it to all samples.
Preserving language and domain distributions when generating synthetic data is crucial for maintaining model generalization.
critical_findings: Training on soft labels generated by a critique model with Chain-of-Thought reasoning performed worse than training without them.
Applying TTA to 100% of samples using a quantized 72B model was significantly worse on the LB than applying it to only 33% of samples with the original model.
Layer pruning based on activation similarity for larger models yielded worse results than simply using a smaller, unpruned model.
Enforcing similar output embeddings before the linear head for response orders did not eliminate the need for a second inference pass.
what_did_not_work: Critique model with CoT for soft labeling.
Layer pruning based on activation similarity.
Enforcing similar output embeddings for response orders.
